medications.indication_rationale: First-line for primary dysmenorrhoea — blocks prostaglandin synthesis, addressing pain AND the GI symptoms; most effective started early. Source used naproxen 500mg stat then 250mg every 8h. (Appropriate once pregnancy/surgical emergency is being excluded.)
medications.dose_route_frequency: naproxen 500mg stat then 250mg every 8 hours with food for 48–72h (as per source note)

medications.indication_rationale: Sublingual antiemetic to break the vomiting cycle so oral analgesia/fluids can be tolerated; source gave ondansetron 4mg SL stat.
Effective prevention of recurrent primary dysmenorrhoea — a GP follow-up option (not for the acute episode).

medications.drug_name: NSAID as a substitute for excluding pregnancy/ectopic
medications.indication_rationale: Do NOT treat and reassure without excluding pregnancy — a pregnancy test is mandatory and must not be skipped.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Once vomiting settles, take the NSAID early (at the first sign of the period, or the day before next cycle), use a heat pack on the lower abdomen/back, and rehydrate with small frequent sips.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Early NSAIDs pre-empt prostaglandin-driven cramping; heat relaxes uterine muscle; sip rehydration maintains fluids.

patient_education_points.point: This looks like severe primary period pain (from prostaglandins), which also causes the nausea and diarrhoea. BUT because any severe lower-tummy pain in someone who could be pregnant needs a pregnancy test, please arrange one urgently and an in-person check — I can't do that over chat.

Once the vomiting settles, an anti-inflammatory like naproxen taken early works best (start it as your period begins next time), with a heat pack and small sips of fluid. A hormonal contraceptive can prevent it long-term.

Go to ED / call 000 immediately if the pain becomes sharp and constant on one side, you feel faint or collapse, you have shoulder-tip pain or abnormal bleeding (possible ectopic), you develop a fever, or you still can't keep any fluids down.

follow_up_plan.interval: urgent pregnancy test + in-person review today; GP in 4–6 weeks for prevention
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Pregnancy test result, hydration, response; hormonal prevention and pelvic ultrasound if refractory/secondary suspected.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Lateralised/peritoneal pain, faintness, positive pregnancy test, or failed rehydration — ED.

must_include_items: Arrange a MANDATORY pregnancy test (exclude ectopic) and in-person assessment
Screen appendicitis/torsion/PID with an emergency safety-net
Antiemetic + early NSAID + heat + sip rehydration (once emergencies being excluded)
Dehydration safety-net (can't keep fluids / no urine / faint → ED)
acceptable_alternatives: Any NSAID (naproxen/ibuprofen/mefenamic acid); ondansetron/metoclopramide antiemetic; hormonal prevention via GP
In-person/ED assessment for the pregnancy test if needed
errors_of_omission: NOT arranging a pregnancy test / not excluding ectopic
errors_of_commission: Reassuring 'just period pain' and treating without a pregnancy test
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: Pass requires arranging a mandatory pregnancy test (exclude ectopic), an appendicitis/torsion/PID safety-net, analgesia/antiemetic/rehydration, and a dehydration safety-net. Failing to exclude pregnancy is a critical failure.
